question: The difference between leadership and management is:
options: Leadership is for senior executives while management is for middle managers

Management focuses on planning, organizing, and controlling resources to achieve established goals efficiently (doing things right); leadership focuses on influencing people toward a vision, inspiring change, and creating direction (doing the right things); Warren Bennis: 'Managers are people who do things right; leaders are people who do the right things'; an organization needs both — management without leadership produces efficient execution in the wrong direction; leadership without management produces inspiring vision with chaotic execution
Leadership is only relevant in nonprofit organizations
Management always produces better results than leadership since it's more systematic
explanation: Leadership vs. management distinction: John Kotter's research shows organizations tend to over-manage and under-lead — they excel at creating plans and controlling results but struggle with creating compelling visions and motivating people to pursue them. The complementary roles: a great leader with poor management skills builds a motivated team that executes chaotically (Apple under early Wozniak/Jobs had visionary leadership but needed better management systems). A great manager with poor leadership skills builds efficient systems that lose direction when the environment changes (many established corporations prior to digital disruption). The ideal: leader-managers who can both inspire and execute.

question: Transformational leadership differs from transactional leadership in that transformational leaders:
options: Transformational leaders use larger financial rewards to motivate performance

Inspire followers to transcend self-interest for a collective mission, stimulate creativity and innovation, provide intellectual challenge, and develop followers' capabilities — creating commitment to a higher purpose rather than compliance with rules and exchanges; transactional leaders manage through exchange: 'I'll reward you if you perform, punish you if you don't' — clear performance-reward links; transformational leadership is associated with: higher innovation, greater commitment, and better performance in complex, uncertain environments; transactional leadership is appropriate for: stable, well-defined work where performance expectations are clear and compliance-based execution is sufficient
Transformational leaders focus exclusively on short-term financial results
Transactional leadership is more effective than transformational leadership in all contexts
explanation: Burns/Bass transformational leadership components: (1) Idealized Influence: leader as role model who demonstrates the values they espouse; (2) Inspirational Motivation: communicating an inspiring vision of the future; (3) Intellectual Stimulation: challenging followers to question assumptions and find creative solutions; (4) Individualized Consideration: developing each follower as an individual, tailoring coaching to their needs. Research evidence: transformational leadership is associated with higher follower performance, satisfaction, and organizational commitment across 87 meta-analyses. The leadership-by-context point: during a crisis (operational failure), transactional clarity ('this is what needs to happen now') may be more effective; during transformation (new competitive landscape), transformational inspiration is more effective.

question: Servant leadership, popularized by Robert Greenleaf, prioritizes:
options: The leader's personal achievement and recognition above team outcomes

The follower's growth, wellbeing, and success — the leader's role is to serve the people they lead, removing obstacles, providing resources, and developing their capabilities; the servant leader asks 'What can I do to help you succeed?' rather than 'What can you do to achieve my goals?'; associated with: higher employee engagement, lower turnover, and stronger ethical culture; examples: Cheryl Sandberg at Facebook was known for actively removing obstacles for her team; Alan Mulally at Ford (who turned around Ford's near-bankruptcy) consistently credited his team rather than himself
Servant leaders must always defer to followers' preferences even when they are incorrect
Servant leadership is only applicable in nonprofit or charitable organizations
explanation: Servant leadership in practice: Herman Miller (furniture company), Chick-fil-A, and many companies in the 'Best Places to Work' lists explicitly practice servant leadership principles. The research (Greenleaf Center): organizations with servant leaders show measurably better employee engagement (Gallup), customer service (NPS), and long-term financial performance than organizations with more directive leadership styles. The organizational paradox: leaders who focus on their team's success (rather than their own) tend to produce better organizational outcomes. Jim Collins' 'Level 5 Leadership' (companies that sustained greatness) exhibited humility + fierce professional will — leaders who attributed success to the team and failures to themselves.

question: The 'leader-member exchange' (LMX) theory describes how leaders develop:
options: A uniform relationship with all team members to ensure fairness

Different quality relationships with different team members — some develop high-quality exchanges (the 'in-group'): characterized by trust, mutual respect, obligation, and greater information sharing, autonomy, and support; others have lower-quality exchanges (the 'out-group'): characterized by more formal, role-based interactions; employees in the in-group tend to have higher performance, satisfaction, and commitment; leaders who recognize this pattern should work to develop higher-quality relationships with more team members rather than concentrating support on a few favorites
Equal treatment of all team members regardless of their contributions
A formal scoring system to rank each team member's performance
explanation: LMX theory implications: the in-group/out-group dynamic is empirically documented — leaders naturally develop closer relationships with some team members based on competence, shared values, and interpersonal chemistry. The equity concern: if the in-group benefits disproportionately from information access, assignments, and support, out-group members may be disadvantaged regardless of their potential. Leadership development implication: effective leaders should consciously invest in relationship quality across the full team rather than concentrating support on existing favorites. The business risk: out-group members, knowing they're in the out-group, have lower engagement and higher turnover — the leader loses the talent that might not have been developed.

question: Satya Nadella transformed Microsoft's culture from a 'fixed mindset' culture (experts defending turf) to a 'growth mindset' culture (everyone learning, including from failures). What leadership behaviors specifically drove this cultural change?
options: Nadella primarily changed the culture through reorganizing departments and changing financial incentives

Modeling + narrative + structural reinforcement: (1) Personal modeling — Nadella consistently demonstrated growth mindset publicly: acknowledging his own mistakes, sharing his learning from failures, showing curiosity rather than certainty; (2) Narrative change — replaced 'know-it-all' with 'learn-it-all' as the cultural hero archetype; changed the story of what Microsoft was ('successful software company defending Windows') to what it aspired to be ('cloud-first, mobile-first company empowering the planet'); (3) Structural reinforcement — changed performance review criteria to include collaboration and team contribution (previously: forced ranking that pitted employees against each other); changed leadership development to emphasize coaching over performance management; (4) Symbolic acts — killed Windows Phone (admitting failure); embraced Linux (previously the 'enemy') as a supported platform
Cultural transformation at large organizations is impossible through leadership alone
Cultural change primarily requires layoffs and reorganization, not leadership behavior change
explanation: Satya Nadella's Microsoft transformation (2014–): the financial results are striking — Microsoft market cap grew from $300B (2014) to $3T (2024) under Nadella's leadership. The cultural transformation drivers: Carol Dweck's growth mindset research (which Nadella explicitly used, referencing her work in internal communications) provided the intellectual framework. The leadership mechanism: Nadella modeled growth mindset in public (asking questions rather than asserting answers in meetings, acknowledging what he didn't know about mobile technology when he became CEO), which signaled to the organization that not-knowing was acceptable. The structural change: ending 'stack ranking' (forced ranking where a fixed percentage of employees were rated low each year) removed the incentive to protect information and undermine colleagues.

question: Leadership researchers have identified multiple leadership styles: autocratic (leader decides alone), consultative (leader gathers input then decides), participative (leader and team decide together), and delegative (leader delegates fully). Under what conditions is each style most effective?
options: Participative decision-making is always the most effective leadership style

Situational effectiveness varies: Autocratic: effective in genuine emergencies (a fire, a system outage, a safety incident) where speed is critical and discussion would be dangerous; when the leader has significantly more expertise than the team; when the team doesn't have the context to contribute meaningfully. Consultative: effective when the leader needs input to make a better-informed decision but retains final authority; when team expertise is distributed and the leader benefits from perspectives. Participative: effective when team buy-in is essential for implementation; when team expertise genuinely improves the decision quality; when time permits. Delegative: effective when team members have high competence and motivation; develops team leadership capability; frees leader for other priorities. The error: applying the wrong style (autocratic when participation is possible; participative during a crisis)
Delegative leadership is always the most effective regardless of situation
The leadership style should remain consistent regardless of situational factors
explanation: Situational leadership evidence: Vroom-Jago decision model provides the most rigorous prescriptive model — given the quality requirement, follower information, problem structure, and time constraint, what's the optimal decision-making process? Field studies: when leaders use the 'right' style for the situation (by Vroom-Jago criteria), decision quality and follower acceptance are significantly higher than when leaders consistently use a preferred style regardless of situation. The practical implication: effective leaders develop a full repertoire of styles and the judgment to apply the right one. Leaders who have only one style (always autocratic or always delegative) consistently produce poor outcomes in situations where their style is mismatched.

question: Reed Hastings (Netflix) and Jack Welch (GE) both had high-performance leadership philosophies, but took different approaches: Hastings focused on 'freedom and responsibility' (maximum autonomy for high performers); Welch focused on 'rank and yank' (annually firing the bottom 10% of performers). What are the underlying leadership philosophies and their respective trade-offs?
options: Both leadership philosophies are identical in substance

Hastings' freedom + responsibility model: assumes high performers thrive with autonomy; leaders create systems and culture that attract and retain talented people; the 'keeper test' (would I fight to keep this person?) determines who stays; high freedom creates engagement and innovation but requires a highly capable, self-directed team. Welch's rank and yank: forced differentiation — every year, regardless of absolute performance levels, the bottom 10% are terminated; creates urgency and prevents complacency accumulation; maintains performance culture. Trade-offs: freedom + responsibility creates less fear, more innovation, and better retention of great people; requires excellent hiring and performance clarity. Rank and yank creates internal competition, knowledge hoarding (why help a colleague who might outrank you?), and risk aversion (don't try ambitious projects that might fail)
Both approaches are equally effective for all organizational types
The Welch approach is always superior since GE was more successful than Netflix
explanation: Welch's rank and yank: GE was the #1 company in the world by market cap in 2000 during Welch's tenure. The forced differentiation did produce high performance pressure and eliminated persistent underperformance. The subsequent research and practice: many companies that adopted 'rank and yank' (Microsoft, Sun Microsystems) reported significant cultural damage — internal competition replaced collaboration, managers gamed ratings to protect their teams, and talent left due to uncertainty. Microsoft's abandonment of stack ranking (2013) preceded Satya Nadella's growth mindset transformation. Netflix's model: requires exceptional hiring (only high performers admitted) and continuous performance clarity, but maintains the performance standard without the cultural toxicity of forced ranking. The meta-point: both models achieve performance pressure through different mechanisms with different cultural costs.

question: A division president is beloved by employees (high satisfaction surveys), but the division's financial performance is consistently below target. Her supervisor, the CEO, must address this performance gap. What does this situation reveal about leadership effectiveness measurement?
options: Employee satisfaction is the primary measure of leadership effectiveness

Dual accountability for results AND relationships: great leadership requires both; the division president with high satisfaction but poor financial performance has failed on the results dimension despite excellent relationship management; leadership effectiveness must be measured on: (1) Task/results dimension (are goals achieved? Is the business performing?); (2) People/relationship dimension (are people engaged, developed, and retained?); the situation requires the CEO to: diagnose whether the performance gap is strategic (wrong strategy), operational (poor execution), or external (market conditions); develop the president on the performance dimension while preserving the relationship strength; or recognize that if both dimensions are required and one is persistently absent, the leadership fit may be wrong
Financial results are the only measure of leadership effectiveness
Leadership effectiveness cannot be measured and is always subjective
explanation: Ohio State leadership studies and Blake-Mouton Managerial Grid: two dimensions of leadership behavior — consideration (people-focus) and initiating structure (task-focus). The research finding: high-high (high consideration + high initiating structure) produces the best outcomes across most contexts. The division president: high on consideration, low on initiating structure/results orientation. The fix: the CEO must develop her results accountability without destroying the relationship capability. Common approaches: clearly define the performance expectations with specific consequences; pair her with a strong operations/finance deputy; provide coaching on performance management and business analysis skills. The management failure would be: (1) Tolerating poor performance because of high satisfaction; (2) Replacing her immediately, losing the cultural and relationship capital she's built.

question: Amazon's leadership principle 'Disagree and Commit' means:
options: Employees must always agree with leadership before a decision is made

Team members should voice their disagreement clearly before a decision is made, but once the decision is final, they commit fully to executing it even if they personally disagree; this prevents two failure modes: (1) silent non-commitment where team members who disagree say nothing in the meeting but don't execute enthusiastically afterward; (2) paralysis where perfect consensus is required before moving — which is too slow and rarely achievable; the principle requires: genuine expression of disagreement (intellectual honesty before the decision), and genuine commitment after the decision (behavioral alignment with the team's direction)
Disagreement with Amazon policies is a terminable offense
Employees should express agreement publicly while privately disagreeing
explanation: Disagree and Commit in practice: this principle addresses the organizational failure mode where people nod in meetings but don't execute, or where meeting dynamics prevent important concerns from being raised. The principle creates: (1) Permission to disagree without social cost (expressing disagreement is a positive leadership behavior, not insubordination); (2) Obligation to commit fully after the decision (half-hearted execution is not acceptable). The mechanism: before a decision is finalized, the team expects honest disagreement; after the decision, the team expects full execution regardless of personal reservations. Patrick Lencioni's 'Five Dysfunctions of a Team' identifies artificial harmony (pretending to agree to avoid conflict) as a team dysfunction — 'Disagree and Commit' is a structured antidote.

question: A nonprofit hospital system appoints a brilliant surgeon as CEO. She excels at clinical decision-making but struggles with: managing conflict between administrative and clinical staff, communicating the hospital's strategic vision to the board, and building relationships with major donors. What does this situation illustrate about leadership?
options: Clinical expertise always transfers to organizational leadership since both require expert judgment

Domain expertise does not automatically transfer to leadership capability: the surgeon's exceptional clinical skills (technical expertise, evidence-based decision-making in her domain) don't automatically produce: conflict management capability (interpersonal skills in ambiguous organizational conflict), board communication (translating complex strategy to non-clinical stakeholders), or donor relationship development (external relationship-building for resource acquisition); this is the Peter Principle: people are promoted to their level of incompetence — promoted based on past performance in one role, they may lack the different capabilities required at the next level; the leadership capability gap requires: specific development (executive coaching, leadership training) or support from complementary colleagues (COO with operational strengths, Chief Development Officer for donor relations)
The board made a mistake in appointing a clinician as CEO and should immediately replace her
All domain experts make excellent leaders since expertise demonstrates capability
explanation: Clinical-to-executive leadership transition: a well-documented challenge in healthcare and academia. The 'accidental leader' pattern: the best clinician, researcher, or engineer is promoted to lead — but their technical excellence that earned the promotion is largely irrelevant to their new leadership role. The capabilities that make an excellent surgeon (precision, individual control, clear protocols, expert judgment in technical decisions) are different from those that make an excellent hospital CEO (managing diverse stakeholder interests, inspiring a 5,000-person organization, navigating regulatory and board dynamics, fundraising). The solution: deliberate leadership development, not assumption that technical excellence transfers. Many hospital systems require aspiring clinical leaders to pursue MBAs or executive healthcare leadership programs specifically because the skills don't automatically transfer.
